content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 16, 2001

G. Michael Gilliland
William S. Haft
Hogan & Hartson, LLP
Columbia Square
555 Thirteenth Street, NW
Washington, D.C. 20014-1109

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-225

Dear Messrs. Gilliland & Haft:

This is in response to your request for advice under the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is limited solely to provisions of the Act,
and should not be taken as advice or an opinion regarding any other areas of the law
potentially raised by your letter. This advice is based upon the facts as provided in your
request letter.² Please note that this letter should not be construed to evaluate any
conduct that has already taken place. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

QUESTION

May your client, Accenture LLP, donate a prize for a raffle to be held as part of
United Way's state employee fundraising campaign?

_________

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are
correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71;
§ 3114.)

File No. A-01-225
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Yes, there is no prohibition against donating the prize, because the prize will be
awarded in the context of a bona fide competition and thus, under the Act is not gift to
the employee winning the prize.

FACTS

Accenture LLP ("Accenture") would like to donate a "Match Play Golf
Tournament" gift package to the United Way chapter of California as a grand prize for
the state employee fundraising campaign. The winner and a guest will receive round-trip
airfare, ground transportation, hotel accommodations, tournament credentials and
hospitality, golf passes, optional activities, dinner and entertainment for the Accenture
Match Play Championship to be held at the La Costa Resort and Spa, February 20-24,
2002 in Carlsbad, California. The gift package is valued at $15,000.

It is Accenture's understanding that the United Way fundraiser campaign targets
all state employees regardless of rank, title, or position, and that the raffle drawing is a
regular part of the fundraiser. Any state employee can qualify for the raffle by
contributing a threshold amount to the fundraiser that is determined in proportion to the
individual's salary. It is Accenture's understanding that each person who donates at least
the minimum has one chance to win regardless of the amount by which the contribution
might exceed the threshold amount. The prize will be awarded in a random drawing.

Accenture will help publicize the event and its sponsorship with posters and by
supporting local media attention. Accenture representatives will participate in kick-off
events organized by the United Way and may attend selected "pep rally" style events
throughout the campaign. Accenture will not participate in the operation of the raffle
contest or the selection of the prizewinner.

ANALYSIS

The Act provides that "[n]o elected state officer, elected officer of a local
government agency, or other individual specified in Section 87200 [of the Act] shall
accept gifts from any single source in a calendar year with a total value of more than
$320." (Section 89503.) This same prohibition applies to any member of a state board or
commission and to designated employees of a state agency. (Ibid.) Accordingly, such an
official would be precluded from receiving any gift from a single source valued at more
than $320. Section 89521 prohibits any person from making a gift to an official in
violation of these provisions.

There are exceptions to the definition of a gift. One such exception occurs when a
prize or award is given in a "bona fide competition." (Reg. 18946.5.) If the prize or
award is received in a bona fide competition "not related to the recipient's status as an
official or candidate," the prize or award is reported as income, rather than a gift. (Ibid.)
Assuming that the raffle will be conducted in such a way that the winner of the prize is

File No. A-01-225
Page No. 3

selected by random chance, it would be a "bona fide competition." (Nemeroff Advice
Letter, No. A-99-148; see also Allen Advice Letter, No. A-95-148.)

The more difficult issue in your inquiry is whether the competition is "not related
to the recipient's status as an official or candidate." One of the main factors in making
this determination is the pool of contestants in the competition. In the Burns Advice
Letter, No. A-96-324, we advised that where the contest was open to the public and
included both public officials and other persons who were not public officials, a prize
awarded to a public official in a random drawing was not a gift. In a similar vein, in the
Nemeroff Advice Letter, supra, we advised that a raffle was not related to a contestant's
status as a public official where the contest was open to all, but included state legislators,
legislative staff, exempt appointees, and state civil service employees.

However, in the Allen Advice Letter, supra, No. A-95-148, we advised that a
prize awarded in a contest that was only open to county auditors and staff members of the
State Controller's Office, who were primarily public officials, constituted a gift. In the
Zerovnik Advice Letter, No. I-00-135, we also advised that a raffle held at a conference
for school business officers that was not open to the public was not a bona fide
competition under Regulation 18946.5.

In your fact pattern, the United Way Campaign³ random drawing is open to all
state employees regardless of rank or title. As such, the potential contestant pool would
include public officials, designated employees and nondesignated employees. While we
have not advised in the context of a bona fide competition, whether a contest open to all
state employees is "not related to the recipient's status as an official," we have done so in
the very similar context of discounts under § 82028(a). (In re Russel (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
191.) In this regard, the Commission stated:

"After considering the language and purposes of the
Political Reform Act, we conclude that a discount made available
on a uniform basis to all state employees is a discount available to
members of the public without regard to official status.
Accordingly, such a discount is not income and is not subject to
disclosure under the Political Reform Act.

"Income disclosure is required so that assets and income of
public officials which may be materially affected by their official
actions will be disclosed. Section 81002(d). Discounts are included
in the definition of income so that officials who are offered special
reductions when they purchase goods and services will disclose
those transactions for public scrutiny. However, many discounts
are offered to all members of the public and do not create any
potential for improper influence. Requiring the disclosure of all
discounts would impose burdensome reporting requirements

³ As it is immaterial to our analysis of whether the prize would constitute a gift, we do not reach
the issue of whether Accenture or the United Way Campaign is the donor of the prize.

File No. A-01-225
Page No. 4

without serving a legitimate public purpose. Consequently, the
statutory definition of 'income' excludes discounts which are made
available to members of the public without regard to their official
status."

Arguably, the words 'official status' could refer to an official's
status as a state employee. However, the Political Reform Act is
concerned with disclosure of income that may be affected by a
public official's 'official actions,' and official actions are those acts
done in one's official capacity under color and by virtue of one's
office. [Citation] We think that the purposes of the Political
Reform Act are best served by interpreting the words 'official
status' to refer to the capacity in which one performs official
actions. We conclude, therefore, that the 'official status' of
state legislators is their status as elected state officers, not their
status as state employees. Consequently, a discount that is
available to all employees of the State of California, without
regard to what office or position they hold in state government,
is a discount made available 'without regard to official
status.'" [Emphasis added.]

The same policy considerations that led the Commission to its conclusion in
Russel would apply with equal force in the context of the "bona fide competition"
exception. There is no potential for the prize to be an "improper influence" on any public
official.

Indeed, the competition and attendant random award of the prize are not aimed at
any particular public official or group of public officials, but rather all state employees.
With a group of contestants as "large and heterogeneous" as this, it is tantamount to the
contestant pool being the public. (In re Russel, supra 1 FPPC Ops. 191.)

This conclusion is further supported by the Commission's recent opinion
regarding a state senator's receipt of the John F. Kennedy Profile in Courage Award.
(In re Solis (2000) 14 FPPC Ops. 7.) Although Solis is factually distinct in some respects
(because the pool considered by the Commission in that opinion was composed of a
group including more than public officials), both Solis and your facts involve pools made
up of a very large number of potential recipients. The existence of a broad pool of
contestants is an important factor in application of the "bona fide competition" exception.

Accordingly, based upon the facts presented pertaining to this year's United Way
campaign drive, Accenture may donate the gift package to the United Way for use in its
state employee fundraising campaign drive as a prize available to all state employees who
choose to participate, and this prize when awarded will not be considered a gift under the
Act.¹

¹ The receipt of the package would still constitute income to the recipient and be subject to
applicable reporting and disqualification requirements. (Sects. 87207, 87302(b) and 87103.)

File No. A-01-225
Page No. 5

If you have any questions, or wish to discuss this further, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[Signature]

By: William L. Williams, Jr.
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
